dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for heart problems/AICD at the ACC in Richmond.
Hi, my name is Alex, and I'm an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I've been having chest pain since yesterday, and my primary care clinic sent me over here to VCU.
Got it. May I check how long the chest discomfort has been ongoing?
It's been going on since about 3:00 PM yesterday. It's really bothering me, and I couldn't ignore it anymore.
Can you open your eyes normally when I ask you to?
What is the date today, and where are we?
It's the 21st of January. I believe we are in the ACC.
GCS is 15-eye 4, verbal 5, motor 6. Patient is alert and oriented x4.
Are you in any current distress, like trouble breathing or speaking?
No, I'm sitting upright here, but it's really hard to focus on anything with the pain.
Good. Your airway is clear and patent while you are upright. Do you have trouble breathing now?
Just a little bit earlier-maybe due to the pain.
Respirations are steady at 12. Bilateral breath sounds are clear and equal.
Got it. Your respiration rate is 12, which is within normal limits.
Radial pulse is 76, strong and regular. Skin is normal in color, feels warm and dry.
Can you describe what you were doing when the chest pain first started?
I was sitting at my desk at work, and I haven't done anything particularly strenuous over the last 48 hours. Thought it was my back at first.
Got it. Can you tell me if you take any medications regularly, and do you have any history of medical conditions?
Pantoprazole, some vitamin B6, cyanocobalamin, and rivaroxaban. I've had a history of DVT but no previous chest pain with that.
What about any other relevant medical history or surgeries?
I have a history of anemia.
Any allergies to medications or other substances?
I'm allergic to morphine sulfate with mild reactions.
When did you eat or drink last time?
I had some small food at my office around noon today, I think.
When did you first notice the chest pain?
I noticed it when I was sitting at my desk yesterday. It never really went away.
Does the pain spread or travel to anywhere else, like your arms or neck?
No, it stays right here. It's fairly constant and sharp.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how would you rate the pain right now?
A 9. It feels sharp, like a knife going through the middle of my chest.
Does anything make the pain worse or better?
Not that I've noticed. It just stays the same all day.
Are you short of breath now, or was it earlier?
Not really now. Earlier, maybe I thought I was.
Any nausea or vomiting?
No, I'm not feeling that at all.
Have you vomited at all since the pain started?
No, I haven't vomited.
Have you felt dizzy or lightheaded since the pain started?
No, never felt that.
That's good. You haven't mentioned any vomiting or blood in your stool?
No, just the pain in the chest.
Your lung sounds are clear. Everything on both sides sounds normal.
We tried to place an IV in your arm but were not able to. Any unusual numbness or pain there?
Not from the arm. Just chest pain. No numbness in my legs or arms.
Pulse-76, regular.
Respirations-12, normal in depth.
Blood Pressure-159 over 83.
Blood Glucose-135 mg/dL.
EKG-sinus rhythm, no ST segment abnormalities seen on 4-lead monitor.
We're going to attach you for a 4 lead EKG now.
CV - EKG - 4 lead monitoring performed.
It's also part of the protocol to give you aspirin to help reduce cardiac risk.
ASA 324 mg oral administered now.
We're now going to do a 12-lead EKG for more detailed assessment.
CV - EKG - 12 lead obtained successfully.
We are following protocol 2-1 for chest pain with suspected cardiac causes.
Pulse now 65. Respirations-17. Blood Pressure-158 over 84. Spo2-99%. EKG still shows sinus rhythm.
Blood glucose is undetectable now.
We'll be transporting you non-emergently to VCU ED for further evaluation. Is that acceptable?
Securing you on the stretcher using a five-point harness now.
You are now ready for transport. We'll be with you until the emergency staff接手.
Patient condition did not substantially change during transport. Transport was non-emergent.
No worsening of symptoms or significant changes. No additional interventions given.
We are placing you on bed 21 and giving a full report to staff RN.
You're now in their care. Is there anything else you want to share about your symptoms?
The pain is still there. Thank you for the care.
I appreciate your trust. Let the nurses know there was a partial IV start in your extremity vein, the attempt was on the arm or leg.
Part of the care also involved aspirin for suspected AICD issues, and the 12-lead ECG was performed.
He had a history of DVT and anemia. His medications include pantoprazole, Xarelto, vitamin B6, and cyanocobalamin.
No known recent changes in mental status.
We have your baseline vitals recorded, no significant deviations during our time on scene.
No additional interventions required.
The patient remained complaint of sharp chest pain, but pain did not radiate or come with vomiting, dizziness, or significant shortness of breath.
Patient denied nausea and dizziness during the whole interaction.
Blood glucose level taken at 135 initially and undetectable on second reading.
IV start attempted in left forearm but unsuccessful.
ASA administered per protocol for AICD and chest pain protocol 2-1.
4-lead and 12-lead EKGs both done and showed no ST segment abnormalities, sinus rhythm.
No change in mental status during initial and reassessment.
EMT team ensured all baseline data was documented and interpreted.
Patient transported non-emergent to VCU ED for continued evaluation.
Transport was smooth, patient remained stable throughout.
No new interventions or emergent symptoms during transport.
Aspirin, EKGs and documentation are part of standard protocol for cardiac chest pain.
Patient is alert, oriented, and in no apparent distress.
Last food intake reported as around noon at office, no unusual sour or acidic stomach symptoms.
No allergic reactions to medications or beverages during care.
No known lightheadedness, though pain was severe enough to prompt direct care.
Was not short of breath at time of transport.
Initial and reassessment pulse steady, active and palpable, no drop-off from baseline.
Skin remains warm, dry, and normal throughout checkups.
Patient did not report cough or respiratory symptoms in consultation beyond chest discomfort.
No depression or side effects of medications mentioned recently.
History of DVT noted, no surgical complications or reoccurrence.
No recent falls or illnesses that may have exacerbated chest symptoms.
Anemia and DVT history may have been evaluated at initial or primary care.
Medication list complete and cited-no indications of missed therapies.
No patient interventions, beyond education, was needed.
Patient coordinates well with care team and was willing to receive EKG monitoring and education.
No resistance to emergency care seen at all.
EMT team used verbal confirmation and body language to ensure patient understanding.
Pain remained constant rather than waxing or waning.
Patient condition good enough for non-emergent transport but remains a priority patient for ED intake.
No repeat checks for IV site or circulation needed during transport.
All baseline vital signs consistent with prior measures and no history of hypotension or hypoglycemia.
All initial findings were fully reported to the clinical team upon exit.
Asymptomatic beyond chest pain; no shifts in demeanor or comfort during care.
Patient remains unresponsive to manipulations of comparable arm or leg beyond IV site.
Was not endorsing abdominal pain or digestive distress.
Blood pressure consistent with young adult male with no current stroke or renal pathologies evident.
No rashes or visible signs of PV teeth or gum disease, no heme-positive secretions noted.
GCS remained at 15 per EMT team interpretation and recheck.
No abnormal vital signs, equal and symmetrical breath sounds upon primary and secondary check.
Colorimetric and hemodynamic stability preserved throughout resuscitation.
Patient maintained spontaneous respirations with supportive monitoring tools available.
No neurological deficits or sudden shifts post-intervention.
No chest movement discrepancies or unequal expansion seen during breathing check.
Patient's posturing distinctly non-hypoxic and suggests comfort while mildly distressed.
No obstructive or stridorous breathing evident on primary or following assessment.
Siting of past DVT addressed and reviewed during critical NP samples.
Past anemia and current B vitamins intact and balanced from context of information.
No stomach or renal pain contributing to patient's movement.
Conscious during all phases of resuscitation and communicated clearly.
Patient had no difficulty swallowing or speaking during entire encounter.
No visual or auditory complaints, eye function normal during orientation checks.
No musculoskeletal strains or chest injuries reported in initial work history chart.
No documentation of incident or trauma to explain onset of pain per history review.
Final patient status as alerted and cooperating due to EMT team's consistent follow-up and communication.
All interventions and assessments done in a timely manner and well documented.
No limb ischemia or cyanosis, bleeding, or paresthesia reported from IV location.
Skin found unbroken, showing no signs of shock, infection, or allergic response.
No statistical deviations from prior EH baseline during time period of field response.
Care team followed all aspects of EMSP management, from ground to hospital.
Documentation complete and correctly associated with correct field protocols.
Patient ready for hospital intake with all data passed over properly per standing protocol.
No unknown or unresolved issues during field dosing or monitoring changes.
